---
title: "Client Report - Joins"
subtitle: "Unit 3 Task 3"
author: "Sydney Ahlstrom Sanders"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
import sqlite3
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```


```{python}
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# Include and execute your code here
sqlite_file = 'lahmansbaseballdb.sqlite'
# this file must be in the same location as your .qmd or .py file
con = sqlite3.connect(sqlite_file)
```

## QUESTION 1

__Write an SQL query that pulls in the the salaries table and the collegeplaying table (and any other tables you might need) and store them in polars dataframes. In Python Polars, combine the dataframes to create a list of baseball players who attended BYU-Idaho. The new table should contain five columns: playerID, schoolID, salary, yearID and teamID associated with each salary.__

_Multiple players from BYU-Idaho were successfully trained and played at a professional level. The data spans several decades, with salaries ranging from $150k (Matt Lindstrom and Garrett Stephenson in their early years) to as high as $4M in later seasons. Players also were on multiple big teams, including the Phillies, Cardinals, Orioles, and White Sox._

```{python}
salaries = pl.read_database("SELECT * FROM salaries", con)
college_playing = pl.read_database("SELECT * FROM collegeplaying", con)

byui_players = (
    salaries
    .join(college_playing, on="playerID", how="inner")
    .filter(pl.col("schoolID").str.to_lowercase().str.strip_chars() == "idbyuid") 
    .select(["playerID", "schoolID", "salary", "yearID", "teamID"])
    .unique()
)

print(byui_players)
```


## QUESTION 2

1. Pick any two baseball teams and compare them using a metric of your choice (average salary, home runs, number of wins, etc). Be creative! Write an SQL query to read in the table(s) you need, then use polars to manipulate the data. Finally, make a graph using Lets-Plot to visualize the comparison. What do you learn?

_I compared the Yankees and Red Sox, and found that both teams maintained relatively low and similar payrolls through the 80s and 90s, but around 1995, both teams starting rapidly spending more and more each season. While both teams increased, the Yankees tended to have a higher payroll overall. The Yankees were also the first team in the dataset to break $200M in annual payroll._

```{python}
q_compare = "SELECT teamID, yearID, salary FROM salaries WHERE teamID IN ('NYA', 'BOS')"
df_compare = pl.read_database(q_compare, con)

payroll_history = (
    df_compare
    .group_by(["teamID", "yearID"])
    .agg(pl.col("salary").sum().alias("total_payroll"))
    .sort("yearID")
)

p = (ggplot(payroll_history, aes(x='yearID', y='total_payroll', color='teamID')) 
     + geom_line(size=1.5)
     + geom_point()
     + scale_y_continuous(format='$,.0f')
     + labs(title="Historical Payroll Comparison: Yankees vs. Red Sox",
            subtitle="Comparing total annual salaries (1985-Present)",
            x="Year", y="Total Payroll (USD)",
            color="Team")
     + theme_minimal()
     + ggsize(800, 500)
     + theme(
         legend_position='bottom',
         plot_title=element_text(size=18), 
         plot_subtitle=element_text(size=12)
     )
)

p.show()
```
